original_abstract:
This article examines the evolution of integration processes within regional vocational education systems, with a specific focus on the Volga Federal District. The research demonstrates that integration has become a pervasive trend, influencing nearly every component of the educational landscape. This includes the merging of different educational levels, the consolidation of youth and adult training subsystems, and the structural reorganization of educational institutions. Furthermore, the study explores how integration transforms educational outcomes—bridging the competencies of workers, technicians, and engineers—while also reshaping curriculum content and the collective use of regional resources.

The author categorizes various types of integration and establishes the causal links between changes in educational content and organizational restructuring. Analysis shows that the institutional landscape of Russian vocational education has shifted significantly over the past decade. In higher education, this is evidenced by the emergence of National Research and Federal Universities. In pre-degree vocational training, a new multilevel model has solidified, designed to provide seamless pathways for training skilled workers and mid-level specialists. 

By analyzing vertically and horizontally integrated educational complexes, the paper highlights several key socio-economic benefits. Economically, these processes drive efficiency through "economies of scale," resource consolidation, and reduced administrative overhead. Socially, integration enhances the "capitalization" of education by broadening graduate qualifications and fostering both horizontal and vertical mobility, allowing students to more easily navigate and adapt their educational trajectories to meet shifting labor market demands.
humanized_abstract:
In this article, authors look at the development of the integration of regional vocational education systems through the example of the Volga Federal District. Integration has become a governing principle in all the elements of the educational system. Thus, differentiation between various levels of education has been replaced with a unification, the sub-systems of youth and adult vocational training have been merged, and educational institutions have undergone structural transformations, affecting educational results, which mean the bridging of skills gaps between workers, technicians and engineers, and changing curricula and making regional resources collectively available.

Different approaches to integration and the relationships between changes in curricula and structural reform have been identified by the author. The study also identifies the changes that have taken place over the last ten years in the institutional framework of vocational education in Russia. These include the creation of the National Research Universities and the Federal Universities in the sector of higher education, and the new multilevel system of pre-school vocational training for the qualification and requalification of workers and qualified specialists.

Using vertically and horizontally integrated educational complexes as the analytical framework, several positive effects on the socio-economic situation of the population are revealed. For example, the creation of new production functions in education due to the implementation of “economies of scale” leads to economic rationalization and a reduction in personnel. In addition, the process of capitalization of education increases students’ level and skills, which ultimately leads to an increase in horizontal and vertical mobility of students. They can smoothly adjust to the changing conditions of the labour market.
